standards.subject: rla
standards.description: Explain the author's purpose and message

standards.subject: rla
standards.description: Identify and explain figurative language such as simile

standards.subject: rla
standards.description: Use context within a sentence to determine the meaning of words

standards.subject: rla
standards.description: Make inferences and use evidence to support understanding

standards.subject: rla
standards.description: Evaluate details to determine key ideas

standards.subject: rla
standards.description: Use text evidence to support an appropriate response

standards.subject: rla
standards.description: Describe the interaction of characters and changes they undergo

standards.subject: rla
standards.description: Analyze plot elements and the lesson or theme

standards.subject: rla
standards.description: Recognize characteristics and the central idea of informational text

body.text: 1. A rain gauge is a clear tube that catches rainwater during a storm. In the school garden, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Numbers on the side show exactly how much rain fell. Garden helpers check the tube before deciding whether plants need more water. The gauge helps the class save water after a wet week.
3. The tube is tied to a green post near the tomatoes. Students make better watering choices because they measure first.
1. Students in the children's museum can learn a lot from a museum labels. Museum labels are small signs that help visitors understand displays.
2. A good label names the object and explains why it matters. Some labels ask questions that make visitors look more closely. Labels are short so people can read them while standing.
3. One label in the museum has a silver border. Visitors learn more than they would by only looking.
1. Many people pass the nature trail without thinking about a trail markers. Trail markers are signs that help hikers follow a path.
2. Colored arrows show which direction the trail turns. Distance numbers tell hikers how far they have walked. Warning signs point out steep steps or muddy ground.
3. One marker is nailed to a crooked wooden post. Markers make it easier for hikers to stay on the correct trail.
1. A field guide is a book that helps people identify things in nature. In the pond trail, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Short descriptions point out details that separate one kind from another. Indexes help readers find a page quickly. Most of all, pictures show what leaves, tracks, or rocks look like.
3. The guide fits in the side pocket of a backpack. Readers can name what they notice outdoors.
1. Students in the library shelf can learn a lot from a library call numbers. A call number is a label that shows where a library book belongs.

2. Books about similar topics are often placed near one another. Returning books to the correct spot helps the next reader find them. Most of all, letters and numbers on the spine match a section of the shelf.
3. The label is printed on a white sticker. The library stays organized even when many people borrow books.
1. Many people pass the community garden shed without thinking about a garden watering schedules. A watering schedule tells garden helpers when each plant bed needs care.
2. The schedule prevents three helpers from watering the same bed. Notes after heavy rain can tell helpers to skip a day. Most of all, new seedlings may need water more often than older plants.
3. The schedule hangs from a silver clip. Plants get enough water without wasting it.
1. A book repair station keeps torn classroom books from being thrown away too soon. In the library corner, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

2. Clean hands and slow page turns help repaired books last longer. Students place ripped pages in a tray instead of taping them quickly. The librarian uses special tape that will not crack or turn yellow.
3. The repair basket sits beside a blue reading chair. More readers can enjoy the same books all year.
1. Students in the morning news board can learn a lot from a weather maps. A weather map uses symbols to show what is happening in different places.
2. Colors often show warmer and cooler temperatures. Cloud pictures can show where rain may fall. Arrows can show which way wind is moving.
3. The map hangs beside the lunch menu. Readers can understand the forecast quickly.
1. Many people pass the gym hallway without thinking about a water fountains with bottle fillers. A bottle-filling fountain lets students refill water bottles quickly.
2. Cold water helps students stay refreshed after exercise. The tall spout sends water straight into the bottle without spilling. A small counter shows how many plastic bottles were not used.
3. The machine makes a soft humming sound. Students drink more water and throw away fewer bottles.
1. A fire drill map shows the safest path out of a classroom. In the classroom doorway, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Arrows lead from the room to the nearest exit. A meeting spot is marked so the class knows where to gather. Practicing the route helps students move calmly.
3. Everyone can leave the building more safely during a drill. The map is inside a clear plastic sleeve.
1. Students in the playground can learn a lot from a playground shade sails. A shade sail is a strong cloth stretched above part of a playground.
2. The cloth blocks some sunlight during hot parts of the day. Cooler benches give students a better place to rest. Workers pull the cloth tight so rain does not collect in the middle.
3. The playground becomes more comfortable in sunny weather. The shade sail is shaped like a triangle.
1. Many people pass the lab table without thinking about a science notebooks. A science notebook records observations during an investigation.
2. Dates help students compare what changed over time. Drawings can show details that are hard to describe in words. Measurements make observations more exact.
3. Students can use their notes to explain what they learned. The notebook cover has a picture of a rocket.
1. A walking school bus is a group of students who walk to school with adults. In the neighborhood sidewalk, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Bright vests help drivers notice the walkers. Students learn to look left, right, and left again before crossing. Most of all, the group follows the same route and stops at marked corners.
3. The walk becomes safer and more organized for everyone. One volunteer carries a clipboard with a star sticker on it.
1. Students in the cafeteria table can learn a lot from a reusable lunch kits. Reusable lunch kits can replace many throwaway bags and wrappers.
2. A cloth napkin creates less trash than a paper one. Families can pack only the food a student expects to eat. Most of all, a washable container can be used again the next day.
3. The trash can fills more slowly during lunch. Some lunch kits have bright stripes.
1. Many people pass the teacher's desk without thinking about a class newsletters. A class newsletter shares school news with families.

2. Short captions explain photographs from class activities. A calendar box helps families remember upcoming events. Most of all, headings divide the page into topics such as dates, projects, and reminders.
3. Families can talk with students about what is happening at school. The newsletter is printed on pale blue paper.
1. A recipe card gives directions for making a food or drink. In the after-school kitchen, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. A serving size tells how many people the recipe will feed. The ingredient list tells cooks what supplies to gather first. Numbered steps show the order for mixing and cooking.
3. Cooks can follow the card instead of guessing. A small drawing of a spoon decorates the corner.
1. Students in the science corner can learn a lot from a classroom aquariums. A classroom aquarium lets students observe a small water habitat.
2. A thermometer shows whether the water is the right temperature. A filter helps keep the water clean for the fish. Students measure food carefully so extra flakes do not cloud the water.
3. The aquarium teaches responsibility and observation. Smooth blue stones cover the bottom of the tank.
1. Many people pass the community center without thinking about a community bulletin boards. A community bulletin board shares notices about events and services.
2. Old flyers are removed so the board stays useful. Flyers tell readers where and when activities will happen. Large titles help people choose which notices to read first.
3. Neighbors can learn about helpful opportunities nearby. Pushpins are kept in a small plastic cup.
1. A compost bin turns fruit peels and old leaves into food for soil. In the cafeteria patio, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Tiny living things break the scraps into dark, crumbly compost. The compost adds nutrients that help plants grow strong roots. Keeping meat and plastic out of the bin prevents bad smells.
3. The lid makes a soft thump when students close it. Over time, less trash leaves the cafeteria, and the garden gets healthier soil.
1. Students in the busy corner can learn a lot from a crosswalk signals. Crosswalk signals help walkers know when it is safer to cross.
2. A white walking symbol tells people they may start across. A flashing hand warns people not to begin crossing. Some signals beep so people can hear the change.
3. The pole has a small sticker near the button. Over time, the signals help walkers and drivers understand each other.
1. Many people pass the transit stop without thinking about a bus route signs. Bus route signs help riders know which bus will stop there.
2. The route number matches the number on the front of the bus. A schedule shows when the bus is expected to arrive. A map shows the streets the bus will travel.
3. The sign is attached to a round metal pole. Over time, riders can plan trips without guessing.
1. A class vote lets students choose between two or more fair options. In the morning meeting rug, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. A tally chart makes the result easy to count. The group agrees to follow the option with the most votes. Most of all, each student gets one vote so the choice is equal.
3. The tally marks are written with a purple marker. Over time, the class can make a decision without arguing for a long time.
1. Students in the art room can learn a lot from a paper-making workshops. In a paper-making workshop, scraps can become a new sheet of paper.
2. A screen lifts the pulp from the water in a thin layer. Pressing out extra water helps the sheet dry flat. Most of all, soaked scraps are blended into a soft pulp.
3. The drying rack sits near a sunny window. Over time, students see how recycling can create something useful.
1. Many people pass the library desk without thinking about a library suggestion boxes. A suggestion box gives readers a way to recommend books or changes.
2. The librarian reads the slips before ordering new books. Several similar suggestions can show what many readers want. Most of all, students write ideas on slips and place them through the slot.
3. The box has a tiny lock on the side. Over time, readers feel that their ideas can improve the library.
1. A classroom job chart lists helpful tasks students can do each week. In the third-grade classroom, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. The chart also reminds students that a classroom belongs to everyone. Rotating the names gives every student a fair turn. Jobs such as line leader and supply helper keep the room running smoothly.
3. The chart has yellow stars beside Friday's jobs. Over time, when students share responsibilities, fewer reminders are needed.
1. Students in the school hallway can learn a lot from a school recycling teams. A school recycling team collects paper and bottles from classroom bins.
2. The team weighs the bags to track progress each month. Team members sort materials so each kind goes to the right place. Posters remind students to empty bottles before recycling them.
3. Their cart has a squeaky front wheel. Over time, the school throws away less reusable material.
1. Many people pass the homework folder without thinking about a reading logs. A reading log is a chart where students record what they read.
2. Dates show how reading habits grow over time. Writing the title helps students remember each book. A short note about the favorite part helps readers think about meaning.
3. The log has a tiny pencil picture in the corner. Over time, students can look back and notice their reading progress.
1. A solar oven uses sunlight to warm food inside a covered box. In the science table, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Shiny foil reflects light toward the food. Clear plastic traps warm air inside the box. Dark paper helps absorb heat from the sun.
3. The box once held a pair of sneakers. The food warms without using a stove or plug.
1. Students in the front hallway can learn a lot from a school maps. A school map helps visitors find classrooms, offices, and exits.
2. A star often marks the spot where the reader is standing. Symbols show bathrooms, stairs, and the nurse's office. A map key explains what each symbol means.
3. The frame around the map is dark brown. New students and visitors can move around with less confusion.
1. Many people pass the bean patch without thinking about a garden trellises. A garden trellis is a frame that gives climbing plants a place to grow upward.
2. Vines wrap around the poles instead of spreading across the ground. Keeping leaves off wet soil can help plants stay healthier. Trellises also make beans and peas easier to pick.
3. The frame casts a striped shadow at noon. The garden uses space more neatly.
1. A seed library lets people borrow packets of seeds instead of books. In the public library, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

2. Returning saved seeds helps the library offer more choices next spring. Labels tell gardeners how much sun and water each plant needs. Most of all, families plant the seeds and save a few new seeds after harvest.
3. The packets are stored in an old wooden card catalog. The community grows food and flowers while sharing resources.
1. Students in the botanical garden can learn a lot from a plant labels. Plant labels give visitors quick information about flowers, trees, and herbs.
2. Smaller lines may tell where the plant grows naturally. Some labels include pictures for readers who are still learning the words. Most of all, the largest words usually name the plant.
3. A lizard-shaped shadow sometimes falls across the label. Visitors can compare plants without asking a guide each time.
1. Many people pass the butterfly garden without thinking about a garden pollination signs. A pollination sign explains how pollen moves from flower to flower.
2. Arrows point from one blossom to the next. A diagram shows how seeds can form after pollination. Most of all, the sign shows that some insects carry pollen on their bodies.
3. The sign is shaped like a large leaf. Visitors understand why flower visitors matter.
1. A lost-and-found label can help return a missing jacket or lunch box. In the school office, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Checking the rack often keeps the pile from growing too large. A name inside an item tells adults who owns it. A classroom number helps the office send the item to the right place.
3. The rack stands under a window. Labeled items are returned more quickly.
1. Students in the science windowsill can learn a lot from a water-cycle jars. A water-cycle jar is a small model that shows water moving in a cycle.
2. The droplets fall back down when they become heavy. Warm sunlight causes some water to evaporate from the soil. Droplets collect on the plastic wrap like tiny clouds.
3. The jar sits on a folded paper towel. Students can watch a slow version of evaporation and precipitation.
1. Many people pass the science book without thinking about a table of contents pages. A table of contents appears near the beginning of many nonfiction books.
2. Readers can use it to skip directly to the information they need. It lists chapter titles in the order they appear. Page numbers tell readers where each section starts.
3. The page is printed in dark blue ink. Finding a topic becomes faster than flipping through every page.
1. Before a long ride, cyclists can do a quick bike safety check. In the park pavilion, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Squeezing the brakes shows whether the bike can stop quickly. A firm tire rolls more safely than a soft tire. A helmet should sit level and snug on the rider's head.
3. Checking equipment first can prevent problems later. The bike bell may make a cheerful ring.
1. Students in the family hallway closet can learn a lot from an emergency kits. An emergency kit holds supplies a family may need during a power outage.
2. A flashlight helps people move safely in the dark. Bottled water and snacks are useful if stores are closed. A radio can share weather updates when the internet is not working.
3. Families feel more prepared when a storm arrives. The kit is stored in a red backpack.
1. Many people pass the school cafeteria without thinking about a noise meters. A noise meter shows when a room is getting too loud.
2. Green means voices are at a comfortable level. Yellow warns students to lower their voices. Red means the sound is making it hard for people to talk.
3. Students can adjust their voices without waiting for a shout. The meter is mounted near the clock.
1. Morning announcements share important news with the whole school. In the school speaker, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

2. Speaking clearly helps every classroom understand the message. Short announcements keep the school day from starting late. Most of all, the announcer reads reminders about clubs, lunches, and events.
3. Students and teachers begin the day with the same information. The microphone has a black foam cover.
1. Students in the cafeteria line can learn a lot from a cafeteria menu boards. A cafeteria menu board tells students what foods are being served.

2. Pictures can help younger readers choose before they reach the counter. Allergy notes warn students about ingredients such as nuts. Most of all, large headings separate the main dish, sides, fruit, and milk.
3. The line moves faster because students can decide early. A magnet shaped like an apple holds one corner.

3. Students make better watering choices because they measure first. The tube is tied to a green post near the tomatoes.

1. Amara stood in the classroom window with the team banner and wanted to give up and start over. At first, Amara acted proud because the work seemed easier in the morning.

2. Then the materials did not fit the way the directions showed. The materials worked only on the third try. Grandma June noticed a frown that quickly turned thoughtful and said, "Helping someone else can move the whole group forward," while pointing to the part Amara had skipped.

3. Amara spoke slowly even though the words felt stuck after comparing the first try with the second. By the end, the class noticed the effort more than the mistake, and Amara felt more patient than before.

1. Many people pass the botanical garden without thinking about a plant labels. Plant labels give visitors quick information about flowers, trees, and herbs.

3. A lizard-shaped shadow sometimes falls across the label. Visitors can compare plants without asking a guide each time.
1. Bus route signs help riders know which bus will stop there. In the transit stop, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

1. Students in the classroom doorway can learn a lot from a fire drill maps. A fire drill map shows the safest path out of a classroom.

3. The map is inside a clear plastic sleeve. Everyone can leave the building more safely during a drill.
1. Many people pass the school speaker without thinking about a morning announcements. Morning announcements share important news with the whole school.

3. The microphone has a black foam cover. Students and teachers begin the day with the same information.
1. A school map helps visitors find classrooms, offices, and exits. In the front hallway, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. A map key explains what each symbol means. A star often marks the spot where the reader is standing. Symbols show bathrooms, stairs, and the nurse's office.
3. The frame around the map is dark brown. New students and visitors can move around with less confusion.
1. Students in the community center can learn a lot from a community bulletin boards. A community bulletin board shares notices about events and services.
2. Old flyers are removed so the board stays useful. Flyers tell readers where and when activities will happen. Large titles help people choose which notices to read first.
3. Pushpins are kept in a small plastic cup. Neighbors can learn about helpful opportunities nearby.
1. Many people pass the library corner without thinking about a book repair stations. A book repair station keeps torn classroom books from being thrown away too soon.

2. Clean hands and slow page turns help repaired books last longer. Students place ripped pages in a tray instead of taping them quickly. The librarian uses special tape that will not crack or turn yellow.
3. The repair basket sits beside a blue reading chair. More readers can enjoy the same books all year.
1. Before a long ride, cyclists can do a quick bike safety check. In the park pavilion, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Squeezing the brakes shows whether the bike can stop quickly. A firm tire rolls more safely than a soft tire. A helmet should sit level and snug on the rider's head.
3. Checking equipment first can prevent problems later. The bike bell may make a cheerful ring.
1. Students in the school hallway can learn a lot from a school recycling teams. A school recycling team collects paper and bottles from classroom bins.
2. Team members sort materials so each kind goes to the right place. Posters remind students to empty bottles before recycling them. The team weighs the bags to track progress each month.
3. The school throws away less reusable material. Their cart has a squeaky front wheel.
1. Many people pass the teacher's desk without thinking about a class newsletters. A class newsletter shares school news with families.
2. Headings divide the page into topics such as dates, projects, and reminders. Short captions explain photographs from class activities. A calendar box helps families remember upcoming events.
3. Families can talk with students about what is happening at school. The newsletter is printed on pale blue paper.
1. A field guide is a book that helps people identify things in nature. In the pond trail, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Short descriptions point out details that separate one kind from another. Indexes help readers find a page quickly. Most of all, pictures show what leaves, tracks, or rocks look like.
3. Readers can name what they notice outdoors. The guide fits in the side pocket of a backpack.
1. Students in the school office can learn a lot from a lost-and-found labels. A lost-and-found label can help return a missing jacket or lunch box.
2. A classroom number helps the office send the item to the right place. Checking the rack often keeps the pile from growing too large. Most of all, a name inside an item tells adults who owns it.
3. Labeled items are returned more quickly. The rack stands under a window.
1. Many people pass the art room without thinking about a paper-making workshops. In a paper-making workshop, scraps can become a new sheet of paper.
2. A screen lifts the pulp from the water in a thin layer. Pressing out extra water helps the sheet dry flat. Most of all, soaked scraps are blended into a soft pulp.
3. Students see how recycling can create something useful. The drying rack sits near a sunny window.
1. A science notebook records observations during an investigation. In the lab table, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Measurements make observations more exact. Dates help students compare what changed over time. Drawings can show details that are hard to describe in words.
3. Students can use their notes to explain what they learned. The notebook cover has a picture of a rocket.
1. Students in the school garden can learn a lot from a rain gauges. A rain gauge is a clear tube that catches rainwater during a storm.
2. The gauge helps the class save water after a wet week. Numbers on the side show exactly how much rain fell. Garden helpers check the tube before deciding whether plants need more water.
3. Students make better watering choices because they measure first. The tube is tied to a green post near the tomatoes.
1. Many people pass the public library without thinking about a seed libraries. A seed library lets people borrow packets of seeds instead of books.

2. Labels tell gardeners how much sun and water each plant needs. Families plant the seeds and save a few new seeds after harvest. Returning saved seeds helps the library offer more choices next spring.
3. The community grows food and flowers while sharing resources. The packets are stored in an old wooden card catalog.
1. Crosswalk signals help walkers know when it is safer to cross. In the busy corner, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. A white walking symbol tells people they may start across. A flashing hand warns people not to begin crossing. Some signals beep so people can hear the change.
3. The pole has a small sticker near the button. Over time, the signals help walkers and drivers understand each other.
1. Students in the gym hallway can learn a lot from a water fountains with bottle fillers. A bottle-filling fountain lets students refill water bottles quickly.
2. The tall spout sends water straight into the bottle without spilling. A small counter shows how many plastic bottles were not used. Cold water helps students stay refreshed after exercise.
3. The machine makes a soft humming sound. Over time, students drink more water and throw away fewer bottles.
1. Many people pass the school cafeteria without thinking about a noise meters. A noise meter shows when a room is getting too loud.
2. Green means voices are at a comfortable level. Yellow warns students to lower their voices. Red means the sound is making it hard for people to talk.
3. The meter is mounted near the clock. Over time, students can adjust their voices without waiting for a shout.
1. A solar oven uses sunlight to warm food inside a covered box. In the science table, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Clear plastic traps warm air inside the box. Dark paper helps absorb heat from the sun. Most of all, shiny foil reflects light toward the food.
3. The box once held a pair of sneakers. Over time, the food warms without using a stove or plug.
1. Students in the science corner can learn a lot from a classroom aquariums. A classroom aquarium lets students observe a small water habitat.

3. Smooth blue stones cover the bottom of the tank. Over time, the aquarium teaches responsibility and observation.
1. Many people pass the community garden shed without thinking about a garden watering schedules. A watering schedule tells garden helpers when each plant bed needs care.

3. The schedule hangs from a silver clip. Over time, plants get enough water without wasting it.

3. Theo helped a younger student before returning to the task after listening to the quietest teammate. When the group finished with smiles instead of arguments, the lesson of the day was clear.
1. Rina had one goal in the music stage: keep a promise. The plant pot sat ready, but Rina's face showed a quiet feeling.

2. Trouble arrived when the important note went missing. The group had to choose between speed and care. Nobody laughed when they saw a pencil mark around the hardest step. Instead, a new classmate said, "Helping someone else can move the whole group forward,"
3. Rina told the truth about the mistake after testing one small change first. After that, the missing piece finally made sense, and the problem no longer felt too big.
1. Ben reached the bus stop early and placed the model volcano on the table. The plan was to fix a problem before anyone notices, even if that meant working too quickly.

1. A book repair station keeps torn classroom books from being thrown away too soon. In the library corner, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

2. Students place ripped pages in a tray instead of taping them quickly. The librarian uses special tape that will not crack or turn yellow. Clean hands and slow page turns help repaired books last longer.
3. The repair basket sits beside a blue reading chair. More readers can enjoy the same books all year.
1. Students in the homework folder can learn a lot from a reading logs. A reading log is a chart where students record what they read.
2. Writing the title helps students remember each book. A short note about the favorite part helps readers think about meaning. Dates show how reading habits grow over time.
3. The log has a tiny pencil picture in the corner. Students can look back and notice their reading progress.
1. Many people pass the community garden shed without thinking about a garden watering schedules. A watering schedule tells garden helpers when each plant bed needs care.
2. New seedlings may need water more often than older plants. The schedule prevents three helpers from watering the same bed. Notes after heavy rain can tell helpers to skip a day.
3. The schedule hangs from a silver clip. Plants get enough water without wasting it.
1. A school recycling team collects paper and bottles from classroom bins. In the school hallway, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

2. Posters remind students to empty bottles before recycling them. The team weighs the bags to track progress each month. Most of all, team members sort materials so each kind goes to the right place.
3. Their cart has a squeaky front wheel. The school throws away less reusable material.
1. Students in the library shelf can learn a lot from a library call numbers. A call number is a label that shows where a library book belongs.

2. Books about similar topics are often placed near one another. Returning books to the correct spot helps the next reader find them. Most of all, letters and numbers on the spine match a section of the shelf.
3. The label is printed on a white sticker. The library stays organized even when many people borrow books.
1. Many people pass the third-grade classroom without thinking about a classroom job charts. A classroom job chart lists helpful tasks students can do each week.

2. Jobs such as line leader and supply helper keep the room running smoothly. The chart also reminds students that a classroom belongs to everyone. Most of all, rotating the names gives every student a fair turn.
3. The chart has yellow stars beside Friday's jobs. When students share responsibilities, fewer reminders are needed.
1. A field guide is a book that helps people identify things in nature. In the pond trail, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Indexes help readers find a page quickly. Pictures show what leaves, tracks, or rocks look like. Short descriptions point out details that separate one kind from another.
3. The guide fits in the side pocket of a backpack. Readers can name what they notice outdoors.
1. Students in the library desk can learn a lot from a library suggestion boxes. A suggestion box gives readers a way to recommend books or changes.
2. Several similar suggestions can show what many readers want. Students write ideas on slips and place them through the slot. The librarian reads the slips before ordering new books.
3. The box has a tiny lock on the side. Readers feel that their ideas can improve the library.
1. Many people pass the nature trail without thinking about a trail markers. Trail markers are signs that help hikers follow a path.
2. Warning signs point out steep steps or muddy ground. Colored arrows show which direction the trail turns. Distance numbers tell hikers how far they have walked.
3. One marker is nailed to a crooked wooden post. Markers make it easier for hikers to stay on the correct trail.
1. In a paper-making workshop, scraps can become a new sheet of paper. In the art room, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Soaked scraps are blended into a soft pulp. A screen lifts the pulp from the water in a thin layer. Pressing out extra water helps the sheet dry flat.
3. Students see how recycling can create something useful. The drying rack sits near a sunny window.
1. Students in the children's museum can learn a lot from a museum labels. Museum labels are small signs that help visitors understand displays.
2. A good label names the object and explains why it matters. Some labels ask questions that make visitors look more closely. Labels are short so people can read them while standing.
3. Visitors learn more than they would by only looking. One label in the museum has a silver border.
1. Many people pass the morning meeting rug without thinking about a class voting. A class vote lets students choose between two or more fair options.
2. Each student gets one vote so the choice is equal. A tally chart makes the result easy to count. The group agrees to follow the option with the most votes.
3. The class can make a decision without arguing for a long time. The tally marks are written with a purple marker.
1. A rain gauge is a clear tube that catches rainwater during a storm. In the school garden, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

2. Garden helpers check the tube before deciding whether plants need more water. The gauge helps the class save water after a wet week. Most of all, numbers on the side show exactly how much rain fell.
3. Students make better watering choices because they measure first. The tube is tied to a green post near the tomatoes.
1. Students in the transit stop can learn a lot from a bus route signs. Bus route signs help riders know which bus will stop there.
2. A schedule shows when the bus is expected to arrive. A map shows the streets the bus will travel. Most of all, the route number matches the number on the front of the bus.
3. Riders can plan trips without guessing. The sign is attached to a round metal pole.
1. Many people pass the cafeteria line without thinking about a cafeteria menu boards. A cafeteria menu board tells students what foods are being served.

2. Pictures can help younger readers choose before they reach the counter. Allergy notes warn students about ingredients such as nuts. Most of all, large headings separate the main dish, sides, fruit, and milk.
3. The line moves faster because students can decide early. A magnet shaped like an apple holds one corner.
1. Crosswalk signals help walkers know when it is safer to cross. In the busy corner, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Some signals beep so people can hear the change. A white walking symbol tells people they may start across. A flashing hand warns people not to begin crossing.
3. The signals help walkers and drivers understand each other. The pole has a small sticker near the button.
1. Students in the school speaker can learn a lot from a morning announcements. Morning announcements share important news with the whole school.
2. Short announcements keep the school day from starting late. The announcer reads reminders about clubs, lunches, and events. Speaking clearly helps every classroom understand the message.
3. Students and teachers begin the day with the same information. The microphone has a black foam cover.
1. Many people pass the cafeteria patio without thinking about a compost bins. A compost bin turns fruit peels and old leaves into food for soil.
2. Keeping meat and plastic out of the bin prevents bad smells. Tiny living things break the scraps into dark, crumbly compost. The compost adds nutrients that help plants grow strong roots.
3. Less trash leaves the cafeteria, and the garden gets healthier soil. The lid makes a soft thump when students close it.
1. A noise meter shows when a room is getting too loud. In the school cafeteria, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Green means voices are at a comfortable level. Yellow warns students to lower their voices. Red means the sound is making it hard for people to talk.
3. The meter is mounted near the clock. Over time, students can adjust their voices without waiting for a shout.
1. Students in the community center can learn a lot from a community bulletin boards. A community bulletin board shares notices about events and services.
2. Flyers tell readers where and when activities will happen. Large titles help people choose which notices to read first. Old flyers are removed so the board stays useful.
3. Pushpins are kept in a small plastic cup. Over time, neighbors can learn about helpful opportunities nearby.
1. Many people pass the family hallway closet without thinking about an emergency kits. An emergency kit holds supplies a family may need during a power outage.
2. A flashlight helps people move safely in the dark. Bottled water and snacks are useful if stores are closed. A radio can share weather updates when the internet is not working.
3. The kit is stored in a red backpack. Over time, families feel more prepared when a storm arrives.
1. A classroom aquarium lets students observe a small water habitat. In the science corner, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

2. Students measure food carefully so extra flakes do not cloud the water. A thermometer shows whether the water is the right temperature. Most of all, a filter helps keep the water clean for the fish.
3. Smooth blue stones cover the bottom of the tank. Over time, the aquarium teaches responsibility and observation.
1. Students in the park pavilion can learn a lot from a bike safety checks. Before a long ride, cyclists can do a quick bike safety check.
2. A firm tire rolls more safely than a soft tire. A helmet should sit level and snug on the rider's head. Most of all, squeezing the brakes shows whether the bike can stop quickly.
3. The bike bell may make a cheerful ring. Over time, checking equipment first can prevent problems later.
1. Many people pass the after-school kitchen without thinking about a recipe cards. A recipe card gives directions for making a food or drink.
2. Numbered steps show the order for mixing and cooking. A serving size tells how many people the recipe will feed. Most of all, the ingredient list tells cooks what supplies to gather first.
3. A small drawing of a spoon decorates the corner. Over time, cooks can follow the card instead of guessing.
1. A table of contents appears near the beginning of many nonfiction books. In the science book, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Readers can use it to skip directly to the information they need. It lists chapter titles in the order they appear. Page numbers tell readers where each section starts.
3. The page is printed in dark blue ink. Over time, finding a topic becomes faster than flipping through every page.
1. Students in the teacher's desk can learn a lot from a class newsletters. A class newsletter shares school news with families.
2. A calendar box helps families remember upcoming events. Headings divide the page into topics such as dates, projects, and reminders. Short captions explain photographs from class activities.
3. The newsletter is printed on pale blue paper. Over time, families can talk with students about what is happening at school.
1. Many people pass the science windowsill without thinking about a water-cycle jars. A water-cycle jar is a small model that shows water moving in a cycle.
2. The droplets fall back down when they become heavy. Warm sunlight causes some water to evaporate from the soil. Droplets collect on the plastic wrap like tiny clouds.
3. The jar sits on a folded paper towel. Over time, students can watch a slow version of evaporation and precipitation.
1. Reusable lunch kits can replace many throwaway bags and wrappers. In the cafeteria table, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. A washable container can be used again the next day. A cloth napkin creates less trash than a paper one. Families can pack only the food a student expects to eat.
3. Some lunch kits have bright stripes. The trash can fills more slowly during lunch.
1. Students in the school office can learn a lot from a lost-and-found labels. A lost-and-found label can help return a missing jacket or lunch box.
2. A name inside an item tells adults who owns it. A classroom number helps the office send the item to the right place. Checking the rack often keeps the pile from growing too large.
3. The rack stands under a window. Labeled items are returned more quickly.
1. Many people pass the neighborhood sidewalk without thinking about a walking school buses. A walking school bus is a group of students who walk to school with adults.
2. The group follows the same route and stops at marked corners. Bright vests help drivers notice the walkers. Students learn to look left, right, and left again before crossing.
3. One volunteer carries a clipboard with a star sticker on it. The walk becomes safer and more organized for everyone.
1. A pollination sign explains how pollen moves from flower to flower. In the butterfly garden, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Arrows point from one blossom to the next. A diagram shows how seeds can form after pollination. Most of all, the sign shows that some insects carry pollen on their bodies.
3. The sign is shaped like a large leaf. Visitors understand why flower visitors matter.
1. Students in the lab table can learn a lot from a science notebooks. A science notebook records observations during an investigation.
2. Drawings can show details that are hard to describe in words. Measurements make observations more exact. Most of all, dates help students compare what changed over time.
3. The notebook cover has a picture of a rocket. Students can use their notes to explain what they learned.
1. Many people pass the botanical garden without thinking about a plant labels. Plant labels give visitors quick information about flowers, trees, and herbs.
2. Smaller lines may tell where the plant grows naturally. Some labels include pictures for readers who are still learning the words. Most of all, the largest words usually name the plant.
3. A lizard-shaped shadow sometimes falls across the label. Visitors can compare plants without asking a guide each time.
1. A shade sail is a strong cloth stretched above part of a playground. In the playground, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Workers pull the cloth tight so rain does not collect in the middle. The cloth blocks some sunlight during hot parts of the day. Cooler benches give students a better place to rest.
3. The shade sail is shaped like a triangle. The playground becomes more comfortable in sunny weather.
1. Students in the public library can learn a lot from a seed libraries. A seed library lets people borrow packets of seeds instead of books.

2. Labels tell gardeners how much sun and water each plant needs. Families plant the seeds and save a few new seeds after harvest. Returning saved seeds helps the library offer more choices next spring.
3. The packets are stored in an old wooden card catalog. The community grows food and flowers while sharing resources.
1. Many people pass the classroom doorway without thinking about a fire drill maps. A fire drill map shows the safest path out of a classroom.
2. Practicing the route helps students move calmly. Arrows lead from the room to the nearest exit. A meeting spot is marked so the class knows where to gather.
3. The map is inside a clear plastic sleeve. Everyone can leave the building more safely during a drill.
1. A garden trellis is a frame that gives climbing plants a place to grow upward. In the bean patch, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Vines wrap around the poles instead of spreading across the ground. Keeping leaves off wet soil can help plants stay healthier. Trellises also make beans and peas easier to pick.

2. The tall spout sends water straight into the bottle without spilling. A small counter shows how many plastic bottles were not used. Cold water helps students stay refreshed after exercise.
3. Students drink more water and throw away fewer bottles. The machine makes a soft humming sound.
1. A school map helps visitors find classrooms, offices, and exits. In the front hallway, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Symbols show bathrooms, stairs, and the nurse's office. A map key explains what each symbol means. Most of all, a star often marks the spot where the reader is standing.
3. New students and visitors can move around with less confusion. The frame around the map is dark brown.
1. Students in the morning news board can learn a lot from a weather maps. A weather map uses symbols to show what is happening in different places.
2. Arrows can show which way wind is moving. Colors often show warmer and cooler temperatures. Most of all, cloud pictures can show where rain may fall.
3. Readers can understand the forecast quickly. The map hangs beside the lunch menu.
1. Many people pass the science table without thinking about a solar oven boxes. A solar oven uses sunlight to warm food inside a covered box.
2. Clear plastic traps warm air inside the box. Dark paper helps absorb heat from the sun. Most of all, shiny foil reflects light toward the food.
3. The food warms without using a stove or plug. The box once held a pair of sneakers.
1. A book repair station keeps torn classroom books from being thrown away too soon. In the library corner, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

2. Clean hands and slow page turns help repaired books last longer. Students place ripped pages in a tray instead of taping them quickly. The librarian uses special tape that will not crack or turn yellow.
3. More readers can enjoy the same books all year. The repair basket sits beside a blue reading chair.
1. Students in the homework folder can learn a lot from a reading logs. A reading log is a chart where students record what they read.
2. Dates show how reading habits grow over time. Writing the title helps students remember each book. A short note about the favorite part helps readers think about meaning.
3. Students can look back and notice their reading progress. The log has a tiny pencil picture in the corner.
1. Many people pass the community garden shed without thinking about a garden watering schedules. A watering schedule tells garden helpers when each plant bed needs care.
2. Notes after heavy rain can tell helpers to skip a day. New seedlings may need water more often than older plants. The schedule prevents three helpers from watering the same bed.
3. Plants get enough water without wasting it. The schedule hangs from a silver clip.
1. A school recycling team collects paper and bottles from classroom bins. In the school hallway, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Team members sort materials so each kind goes to the right place. Posters remind students to empty bottles before recycling them. The team weighs the bags to track progress each month.
3. Their cart has a squeaky front wheel. Over time, the school throws away less reusable material.
1. Students in the library shelf can learn a lot from a library call numbers. A call number is a label that shows where a library book belongs.
2. Letters and numbers on the spine match a section of the shelf. Books about similar topics are often placed near one another. Returning books to the correct spot helps the next reader find them.
3. The label is printed on a white sticker. Over time, the library stays organized even when many people borrow books.
1. Many people pass the third-grade classroom without thinking about a classroom job charts. A classroom job chart lists helpful tasks students can do each week.
2. Rotating the names gives every student a fair turn. Jobs such as line leader and supply helper keep the room running smoothly. The chart also reminds students that a classroom belongs to everyone.
3. The chart has yellow stars beside Friday's jobs. Over time, when students share responsibilities, fewer reminders are needed.
1. A field guide is a book that helps people identify things in nature. In the pond trail, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Short descriptions point out details that separate one kind from another. Indexes help readers find a page quickly. Most of all, pictures show what leaves, tracks, or rocks look like.
3. The guide fits in the side pocket of a backpack. Over time, readers can name what they notice outdoors.
1. Students in the library desk can learn a lot from a library suggestion boxes. A suggestion box gives readers a way to recommend books or changes.
2. The librarian reads the slips before ordering new books. Several similar suggestions can show what many readers want. Most of all, students write ideas on slips and place them through the slot.
3. The box has a tiny lock on the side. Over time, readers feel that their ideas can improve the library.
1. Many people pass the nature trail without thinking about a trail markers. Trail markers are signs that help hikers follow a path.
2. Distance numbers tell hikers how far they have walked. Warning signs point out steep steps or muddy ground. Most of all, colored arrows show which direction the trail turns.
3. One marker is nailed to a crooked wooden post. Over time, markers make it easier for hikers to stay on the correct trail.
1. In a paper-making workshop, scraps can become a new sheet of paper. In the art room, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Pressing out extra water helps the sheet dry flat. Soaked scraps are blended into a soft pulp. A screen lifts the pulp from the water in a thin layer.
3. The drying rack sits near a sunny window. Over time, students see how recycling can create something useful.
1. Students in the children's museum can learn a lot from a museum labels. Museum labels are small signs that help visitors understand displays.

1. Many people pass the morning meeting rug without thinking about a class voting. A class vote lets students choose between two or more fair options.
2. The group agrees to follow the option with the most votes. Each student gets one vote so the choice is equal. A tally chart makes the result easy to count.
3. The tally marks are written with a purple marker. Over time, the class can make a decision without arguing for a long time.
1. A rain gauge is a clear tube that catches rainwater during a storm. In the school garden, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Numbers on the side show exactly how much rain fell. Garden helpers check the tube before deciding whether plants need more water. The gauge helps the class save water after a wet week.
3. The tube is tied to a green post near the tomatoes. Students make better watering choices because they measure first.
1. Students in the transit stop can learn a lot from a bus route signs. Bus route signs help riders know which bus will stop there.

3. The sign is attached to a round metal pole. Riders can plan trips without guessing.
1. Many people pass the cafeteria line without thinking about a cafeteria menu boards. A cafeteria menu board tells students what foods are being served.

3. A magnet shaped like an apple holds one corner. The line moves faster because students can decide early.
1. Crosswalk signals help walkers know when it is safer to cross. In the busy corner, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.

3. The pole has a small sticker near the button. The signals help walkers and drivers understand each other.
1. Students in the school speaker can learn a lot from a morning announcements. Morning announcements share important news with the whole school.

2. Speaking clearly helps every classroom understand the message. Short announcements keep the school day from starting late. Most of all, the announcer reads reminders about clubs, lunches, and events.
3. The microphone has a black foam cover. Students and teachers begin the day with the same information.
1. Many people pass the cafeteria patio without thinking about a compost bins. A compost bin turns fruit peels and old leaves into food for soil.

2. The compost adds nutrients that help plants grow strong roots. Keeping meat and plastic out of the bin prevents bad smells. Most of all, tiny living things break the scraps into dark, crumbly compost.
3. The lid makes a soft thump when students close it. Less trash leaves the cafeteria, and the garden gets healthier soil.
1. A noise meter shows when a room is getting too loud. In the school cafeteria, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Red means the sound is making it hard for people to talk. Green means voices are at a comfortable level. Yellow warns students to lower their voices.
3. The meter is mounted near the clock. Students can adjust their voices without waiting for a shout.
1. Students in the community center can learn a lot from a community bulletin boards. A community bulletin board shares notices about events and services.
2. Old flyers are removed so the board stays useful. Flyers tell readers where and when activities will happen. Large titles help people choose which notices to read first.
3. Pushpins are kept in a small plastic cup. Neighbors can learn about helpful opportunities nearby.
1. Many people pass the family hallway closet without thinking about an emergency kits. An emergency kit holds supplies a family may need during a power outage.

3. The kit is stored in a red backpack. Families feel more prepared when a storm arrives.

2. Squeezing the brakes shows whether the bike can stop quickly. A firm tire rolls more safely than a soft tire. A helmet should sit level and snug on the rider's head.
3. Checking equipment first can prevent problems later. The bike bell may make a cheerful ring.

3. Cooks can follow the card instead of guessing. A small drawing of a spoon decorates the corner.
1. A table of contents appears near the beginning of many nonfiction books. In the science book, this may seem simple at first, but it has an important job.
2. Page numbers tell readers where each section starts. Readers can use it to skip directly to the information they need. Most of all, it lists chapter titles in the order they appear.
